evals.prompt: We're a $15M DTC apparel brand. Should we get HubSpot?
evals.expected_output: Walks the four CRM archetypes. At $15M pure DTC with no sales motion the answer is archetype 1: Klaviyo + Shopify + warehouse is enough. HubSpot is the most over-CRM'd category and unlikely to add measurable LTV. Conditions the recommendation on whether a wholesale or B2B-adjacent arm exists. Recommends saving the spend.
assertions: Walks the four CRM archetypes
Names archetype 1 as fit for pure DTC under $50M
Recommends against standalone CRM
Calls out Klaviyo + Shopify + warehouse as sufficient
Conditions on absence of wholesale or B2B-adjacent arm
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, single next move

evals.prompt: We're DTC at $25M but we just launched a wholesale program with 15 retailers. Do we need a CRM now?
evals.expected_output: Yes, archetype 2 starts to apply. Walks the wholesale-program path. Recommends HubSpot Starter or Pipedrive sized to the wholesale arm only, not enterprise tooling. Pulls channel revenue split to confirm wholesale share. Names the buyer-rep relationship tracking and account history as the core use cases.
assertions: Identifies archetype 2 as the fit
Recommends HubSpot Starter or Pipedrive at this scale
Pulls modern.sales.by_channel for wholesale share
Names buyer-rep tracking as the core use case
Sizes the CRM to the wholesale arm not the whole business
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, single next move

evals.prompt: Our customer data is in 6 different tools and nobody has the full picture. Where do we start?
evals.expected_output: Walks the single-customer-view design framework. Source of truth per data type: Shopify for orders, Klaviyo for engagement, Gorgias for support, CRM for marketing intent. Email as the practical key. Recommends auditing existing tools, picking sources of truth, and routing all customer events through the integration layer rather than duplicating data.
assertions: Walks the single-customer-view source-of-truth framework
Names email as the practical identity key
Names Shopify as order source of truth
Names Klaviyo as engagement source of truth
Recommends source-of-truth audit before tool consolidation
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, single next move

evals.prompt: Our sales team complains they get bad leads. Marketing complains sales doesn't follow up. How do we fix this?
evals.expected_output: Walks the marketing-sales handoff framework. Recommends a two-way contract with documented SLAs: MQL definition fires automatically, sales has 24h acceptance window, 5 business days for first outreach. Monthly round-trip review of acceptance and rejection reasons. Notes that the lead score must be visible and explainable for sales to trust it.
assertions: Walks the handoff contract framework
Names documented SLA for acceptance and outreach
Recommends monthly round-trip review
Names rejection reason codes for nurture re-entry
Calls out lead score visibility as critical to sales trust
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, single next move

evals.prompt: What lifecycle stages should we set up in our CRM?
evals.expected_output: Walks the lifecycle stages framework. Subscriber/Lead, MQL, SQL, Customer (with subcategories), Champion/Advocate, Lapsed/Churn risk. Each stage has a defined transition trigger. Common mistakes called out: MQL defined by form alone, SQL defined by gut, no Customer stage because customers live in Shopify. Recommends documenting transitions and auditing monthly.
assertions: Lists the six lifecycle stages
Names operational transition triggers per stage
Calls out the common MQL-by-form mistake
Calls out the missing-Customer-stage mistake
Recommends monthly transition rate audit
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, single next move

evals.prompt: How should we design lead scoring?
evals.expected_output: Walks the lead-scoring framework. Behavioral points (high-intent actions, weighted by recency), firmographic points (ICP match), negative scoring (inactivity, bad fit), MQL threshold calibrated to sales-team capacity, quarterly calibration. Recommends keeping the formula visible to sales and reviewing it monthly. Names the explainability-in-three-sentences rule.
assertions: Walks the lead scoring framework
Names behavioral, firmographic, and negative scoring components
Names time-weighting for behavioral signals
Recommends sales-capacity-calibrated MQL threshold
Recommends quarterly calibration
Calls out the explainability rule for sales trust
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, single next move

evals.prompt: A vendor is pitching us Salesforce. We're a $40M DTC with no real sales team. Should we?
evals.expected_output: No. At pure DTC $40M with no sales function, Salesforce is archetype 4 over-spec for archetype 1 needs. Walks the over-CRM diagnosis. The right investment is identity and warehouse work (CDP-adjacent) not enterprise CRM. References customer-data-platform-strategy for the deeper data architecture question. Recommends declining the upsell.
assertions: Identifies Salesforce as over-spec for archetype 1
Names the missing sales function as the disqualifying condition
Recommends warehouse and identity investment instead
References customer-data-platform-strategy
Recommends declining the vendor pitch
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact, single next move
